contradiction_text: Keyword filtering made performance work look far more prevalent than the later validated pipeline supports, creating a sharp tension between naive retrieval and the final dataset construction.
related_claim_texts: Naive keyword filtering was not reliable for identifying performance-related agentic pull requests in this corpus, so the study relied on LLM-based classification and manual recovery to construct the final performance dataset.
disclosure: Keep visible that keyword search and LLM-assisted review produce materially different pictures of the corpus; this supports the method shift, but the final set is still a constructed dataset rather than perfect ground truth.

contradiction_text: The article argues that category-specific review dynamics matter more than raw change size, but that remains a tension rather than a fully resolved explanation because merge-time differences are observed without a demonstrated causal mechanism.
related_claim_texts: Among accepted performance-related pull requests, merge times varied by category, with UI, AI, and Analytics taking longest and Low-level work merging fastest, while change size showed only weak association with merge time.
disclosure: Keep visible that the simple larger-change explanation is weakened, not eliminated; the evidence shows category differences and weak size association, but does not establish why some categories merge slower.

contradiction_text: The performance corpus is presented as broad across repositories, agents, and stack layers, yet the evidence cannot resolve whether that breadth reflects true optimization demand, agent behavior, or the composition and modeling choices of the dataset.
related_claim_texts: The final performance-related pull request set was distributed across many repositories, agents, and software-stack areas rather than concentrated in a narrow optimization niche.
disclosure: Keep visible that breadth is supported as an observed distribution in this corpus, but not as proof of the underlying real-world distribution of all agentic performance work.

contradiction_text: The paper shows that most observed performance work is tied to feature implementation rather than testing or maintenance, which creates tension with any expectation that optimization is mainly a later-stage hardening activity.
related_claim_texts: The final performance-related pull request set was distributed across many repositories, agents, and software-stack areas rather than concentrated in a narrow optimization niche.

Among accepted performance-related pull requests, merge times varied by category, with UI, AI, and Analytics taking longest and Low-level work merging fastest, while change size showed only weak association with merge time.
disclosure: Keep visible that the dataset places much performance work inside feature development flow rather than in a clean downstream optimization phase, but the source does not explain whether this is a property of agentic coding generally or of this corpus specifically.
